mnemonics.title: ER Diagram Components
mnemonics.content: REAN: Rectangle (Entity), Ellipse (Attribute), Diamond (Relationship) - Remember the shapes!

mnemonics.title: Cardinality Types
mnemonics.content: One-One (1:1), One-Many (1:M), Many-Many (M:N) - Think of family: One Parent has Many Children, Children have Many Toys (M:N)

mnemonics.title: Weak Entity Identification
mnemonics.content: WED: Weak Entity Depends on owner - Double Rectangle shows it needs a strong entity to exist

acronyms.usage: ER Diagram is the conceptual model developed by Peter Chen for database design
Unique identifier for entity instances, shown as underlined attribute in ER diagram
Attribute that creates relationship between entities, implemented during table creation
Cardinality where multiple entities on both sides can associate with each other
Cardinality where one entity instance relates to multiple instances of another entity
Cardinality where exactly one entity instance relates to exactly one instance of another entity
SQL commands like CREATE, ALTER used to implement ER design into actual database

visualTips.visualization: Think of entities as NOUNS - Person, Place, Thing, or Event. Draw as rectangles: □
Think of attributes as ADJECTIVES describing entities. Draw as ovals: ◇
Think of relationships as VERBS connecting entities. Draw as diamonds: ◊
The underlined attribute is like a fingerprint - unique identifier! Underline it: 𝔹
Weak entity needs support like a child needs parents. Double border shows dependency: ⧃
Two circles merged = multiple values possible. Like a chain: ∞
Single line = optional (can exist alone). Double line = mandatory (must participate)
Crow's foot (>---) shows 'many', single line (|---) shows 'one'. Read from right to left for meaning

analogies.analogy: Entity Set is like the concept of 'Car' (blueprint), while Entity Instance is like your specific car 'BMW X5' (actual object)
Weak entity is like a dependent - cannot exist on their own, must be linked to the primary person (like spouse depends on primary account holder)
Relationships are like verbs in a sentence: 'Student enrols in Course' - Entity-Relationship-Entity forms a complete thought
Like a mailing address: '123 Main Street, Springfield, IL 62701' - one field but contains multiple meaningful parts
A person's phone numbers - you can have home, work, mobile. Not a single value but a set of values
Derived attribute is like a calculator result - Age is calculated from Date of Birth, doesn't need to be stored separately
Cardinality is like capacity: One bus (entity) can carry Many passengers (entities). That's 1:M relationship
